results.possibleIdentity: Didier de Fontaine (1931–2021) was a prominent materials scientist and engineer known for his pioneering work in computational materials science and alloy theory. Born in Giza, Egypt, to a Belgian diplomat, he spent his early years in Europe and Africa before moving to the United States for graduate studies. He earned his PhD in materials science from Northwestern University in 1967. Following his doctorate, he worked as a postdoctoral researcher at Bell Telephone Laboratories in Murray Hill, New Jersey, during the late 1960s (approximately 1966–1969). While at Bell Labs, he resided in Chatham, New Jersey. His technical expertise spanned physical metallurgy, thermodynamics, and computer simulation, particularly the development of first-principles methods for calculating phase diagrams. In 1969, he joined the faculty at the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA), where he taught for ten years before moving to the University of California, Berkeley in 1979. At Berkeley, he was a Professor of Materials Science and Engineering until his retirement in 1994. Throughout his career, he authored over 220 publications and received numerous honors, including the Turnbull Award from the Materials Research Society and the Hume-Rothery Award from TMS. In his personal life, he was a patron of the arts, supporting organizations such as the Berkeley Repertory Theatre, which aligns with potential interests in collaborations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) during his time at Bell Labs.
